ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Dal Makhani exists; FOODON_03311737 (lentil dish) is the closest defensible parent class. Exact Dal Makhani entry is absent from the FoodOn ontology as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Lentils, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this whole-lentil slow-cooked dish; no specific Dal Makhani composite code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific composite code for Dal Makhani. A00KP (lentils, cooked) is the closest defensible single-ingredient anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Dal Makhani or equivalent Punjabi lentil dish found in the SNOMED CT international release as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of Dal Makhani is approximately 250 g (one serving bowl), typically accompanied by garlic naan or laccha paratha.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using whole black lentil (urad dal) and kidney bean (rajma) composition data combined with standard recipe proportions for butter and cream. Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for urad dal (ID 174276) and kidney beans (ID 175200) to validate macro ranges. The composite dish values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation with approximately 8–10% added fat from butter and cream.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for cooked lentils (GI ~29–32) and kidney beans (GI ~24–29)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Dal Makhani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated from the weighted average of whole black lentils (urad dal, GI ~29–32) and red kidney beans (rajma, GI ~24–29) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The high fat and protein content from butter and cream further attenuates the glycemic response, supporting a GI estimate of 35. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 31.25) / 100 = 10.9 g (moderate GL).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg/100 g; ~875 mg per 250 g serving) and significant saturated fat from butter and cream
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Dal Makhani to occasional consumption, request reduced-salt and reduced-butter preparations, and monitor total sodium intake against the recommended ceiling of 2,000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and high dietary fibre (4.8 g/100 g) support glycaemic control, but calorie density from saturated fat requires portion management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Makhani is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (150–200 g) owing to its low GI and high fibre content; however, the high saturated fat and calorie load from butter and cream should be factored into the overall dietary plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~5.2 g/100 g) from white butter and heavy cream raises LDL cholesterol risk; high sodium further elevates cardiovascular burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Dal Makhani sparingly, preferring home-prepared versions with reduced butter and cream, and should account for its saturated fat contribution within the recommended limit of less than 7% of total daily energy intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, cumin) and plant protein from lentils are beneficial, but high saturated fat from dairy may be pro-inflammatory in some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dal Makhani can be included in the diet of individuals with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate portions; reducing butter and cream content will lower the pro-inflammatory saturated fat load while preserving the beneficial plant protein and spice profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Makhani is a celebrated flagship dish of Punjabi cuisine from North India, widely regarded as one of the richest and most indulgent vegetarian preparations in the Indian culinary tradition. It is made from whole black lentils (urad dal, Vigna mungo) and red kidney beans (rajma, Phaseolus vulgaris), which are soaked overnight and then slow-cooked for six to twelve hours — traditionally on a low charcoal fire — until they achieve a velvety, almost porridge-like consistency. The dish is built on a base of tomato puree, ginger, garlic, and onion, tempered with cumin, coriander, garam masala, and red chilli. It is finished with a generous quantity of white butter (makhan) and heavy cream, which give it its characteristic richness and glossy appearance. A distinctive smoky depth is often imparted through the dhungar technique, in which a piece of live charcoal is placed in the pot and doused with ghee, then sealed briefly to infuse the dish with smoke. Per 100 g, Dal Makhani provides approximately 160 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 9.5 g fat (of which ~5.2 g is saturated), 12.5 g carbohydrate, and 4.8 g dietary fibre. Its low glycemic index (~35) makes it suitable for glycaemic management when consumed in controlled portions. The high sodium content (350 mg/100 g) and saturated fat load are the primary clinical concerns for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Dal Makhani is a staple at Indian weddings, festivals, and restaurant menus across the world, and is typically served with garlic naan, laccha paratha, or steamed basmati rice.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using component-level data for urad dal and rajma, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No single approved-source entry for the composite Dal Makhani dish (with butter and cream) was found; values reflect a recipe-level estimation with moderate confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for whole lentils and kidney beans. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from the known fatty acid profile of butter and cream at the recipe proportion used; water_g is set to -1 as moisture data for the composite dish is not reported in the sources consulted.
notes: 1. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite Dal Makhani dish (with butter and cream at restaurant proportions) is not reported in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. Saturated fat (5.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.6 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from the fatty acid profile of white butter and heavy cream at the recipe proportion used (~8–10% added fat), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central butter entry (ID 173410). 3. Cholesterol is revised to 22.0 mg/100 g (from 0.0 in the input) to reflect the dairy fat content (butter and cream); the input value of 0.0 mg cholesterol is inconsistent with a dish containing significant butter and cream. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dal Makhani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 5. The input portions array contained three identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact composite dish codes exist for Dal Makhani in either ontology as of 2024. 7. This is an Indian (Punjabi) dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Hindi name (दाल मखनी) and Punjabi alias (ਦਾਲ ਮखਣੀ) are included per the input and cultural context.
